title: The Week That Was
gist: A Lawfare weekly roundup surfacing several investor-relevant threads: the Pentagon labeling Anthropic's models a 'supply chain risk' exposes dangerous concentration in a handful of AI firms; Supreme Court rulings are expanding at-will executive removal power over independent agencies; and intelligence politicization at ODNI plus military-in-elections legal ambiguity raise governance and election-integrity risks ahead of the 2026 midterms.

insights.claim: The Department of Defense designated Anthropic's top AI models a 'supply chain risk,' barring Pentagon contractors from working with the company, in a dispute that exposes how dependent government and private sector have become on just a few AI firms.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: A single procurement designation can cut a frontier AI vendor out of defense revenue, showing government AI concentration is now a live regulatory and contract risk.
insights.locator: James Goodwin and Arvind Salem argued

insights.claim: Heavy reliance on a handful of AI companies removes decision-making power from civil servants accountable to the public, according to Goodwin and Salem, framing AI vendor concentration as a governance and accountability problem.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Concentration risk in AI could invite antitrust or procurement diversification mandates that reshape the competitive landscape.
insights.locator: James Goodwin and Arvind Salem argued

insights.claim: Charlie Bullock argues the bipartisan Great American AI Act (GAAIA) discussion draft by Reps. Obernolte and Trahan is the best federal frontier AI safety framework proposed yet but does net harm because of limited whistleblower protections and sweeping state-law preemptions.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Watch GAAIA's preemption clause: broad federal preemption of state AI laws would reshape the compliance map for AI companies nationwide.

insights.claim: Anthropic alleged that Alibaba conducted a major distillation attack using Anthropic's Claude to train its own AI model, a claim discussed in the Seriously Risky Business newsletter.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Model distillation theft claims between U.S. and Chinese AI firms escalate the IP-protection and export-control dimension of the AI race.
insights.locator: In last week's Seriously Risky Business

insights.claim: The Supreme Court's Slaughter decision affirmed sweeping presidential removal powers, but Bednar and Phillips argue that firings dropping multimember commissions below their statutory quorum (as at the Election Assistance Commission) leave them unable to execute statutory duties, potentially making the removals unconstitutional under the Take Care Clause.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: A quorum-based counterargument could constrain the removal power's reach over independent commissions, a swing factor for agency stability.
insights.locator: Nick Bednar and Todd Phillips highlighted

insights.claim: Eric Columbus argues Todd Blanche can serve indefinitely as unconfirmed acting attorney general because courts have read the Attorney General Succession Act's grant of 'all the duties of that office' to the deputy AG as overriding the Federal Vacancies Reform Act and the Appointments Clause.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: A legal pathway for indefinite unconfirmed leadership at DOJ concentrates power and reduces Senate check, relevant to rule-of-law risk premia.

insights.claim: Feinberg and Curlee argue that ODNI's vague authorities, created in 2004 to coordinate a fractured post-9/11 intelligence community and historically kept administrative by norms, now give figures like former DNI Tulsi Gabbard and acting DNI Bill Pulte tools to manipulate intelligence and potentially interfere with elections.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Politicized intelligence coordination is an under-priced governance risk heading into the 2026 midterms.
insights.locator: Michael Feinberg and Julia Curlee traced

insights.claim: Orpett, Roberts, and Voss warn that legal ambiguity in statutes and untested constitutional theories like the 'protective power' could let a determined executive deploy the military during elections before courts could intervene.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Election-period military deployment ambiguity is a tail risk for civil stability and market volatility around the 2026 midterms.
insights.locator: In the second part of their analysis

insights.claim: Kal Clark argues AI is already reducing diagnostic errors in radiology by functioning as a second-look safety layer, addressing a large scale of diagnostic error that institutional barriers had prevented systematic second reviews from catching.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: AI as a diagnostic safety layer is a concrete, deployed healthcare use case with a clear ROI narrative against error costs.
insights.locator: Also on Scaling Laws, Kal Clark joined

insights.claim: A Scaling Laws discussion argues AI can cut administrative red tape and reshape hospital financial infrastructure by attacking the economics of healthcare billing and growing administrative overhead.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Healthcare administrative overhead is a large addressable market for AI automation vendors.
insights.locator: On Scaling Laws, Adi Tantravahi joined

insights.claim: A new Joshua Keating series explores how AI is impacting nuclear command, arms development, and the nonproliferation regime, signaling AI's expansion into the highest-stakes military domain.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: AI integration into nuclear command surfaces a new category of strategic-stability and defense-tech risk.
insights.locator: On Lawfare Daily, Scott R. Anderson sat down

insights.claim: Cory Simpson argues Microsoft's ambiguous legal threats against researcher Nightmare Eclipse, who found unpatched Windows vulnerabilities, exposes an industry-wide breakdown of trust in coordinated vulnerability disclosure between companies and security researchers.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Deteriorating vendor-researcher relations could reduce responsible disclosure and raise unpatched-vulnerability risk across major platforms.
insights.locator: Cory Simpson examined Microsoft's ambiguous threats

insights.claim: Bailey Schiff argues the baseline-knowledge gap on Iran's nuclear capabilities has widened since Iran ended Additional Protocol monitoring in 2021 and after 2025-2026 U.S. and Israeli strikes, making a new memorandum of understanding a critical juncture to restore verification or cement ambiguity for years.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Loss of Iran nuclear verification raises proliferation uncertainty and Middle East geopolitical risk premia.
insights.locator: Bailey Schiff argued that the gap

insights.claim: Pablo Chavez argues Washington should enact statutory constraints on the U.S. government's own ability to interfere with U.S. tech companies' services abroad, responding to European allies' fears that the U.S. could cut off access or collect European users' data.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: European digital-sovereignty anxiety is a structural headwind for U.S. cloud and tech exporters absent legal reassurance.

insights.claim: Mattie Webb argues Trump's exceptional-refugee-status policy for Afrikaners and support of the 'white genocide' myth is undermining U.S. credibility and hardening relations with South Africa, which is using the standoff to burnish its standing as an independent Global South middle power.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: A deteriorating U.S.-South Africa relationship affects Global South alignment and BRICS-adjacent diplomacy.
insights.locator: Mattie C. Webb placed the Trump administration's

insights.claim: Carissa Veliz argues that artificial intelligence is 'the new Oracle of Delphi and tech executives the new prophets,' framing AI-driven prediction as a concentration of societal power that a healthy democracy requires uncertainty to resist.
insights.speaker: Lawfare Blog
insights.so_what: Reframes AI prediction as a power-concentration issue, a lens for anticipating regulatory pushback against predictive AI.
insights.quote: artificial intelligence is the new Oracle of Delphi and tech executives the new prophets
insights.locator: On Lawfare Daily, Tyler McBrien sat down with Carissa
